nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs; benthopelagic at depths of roughly 8–35 m

diet.notes: Primarily a grazer of macroalgae and microalgae. Generally advised to offer dried marine seaweed (nori) and spirulina-based foods at least three times per week, supplemented with high-quality herbivore pellet or flake foods. Has been documented accepting meaty protein foods (mysis, krill, enriched frozen preparations) alongside other tank inhabitants, but vegetable matter should dominate the diet to support immune function and reduce aggression.

summary: A jet-black Zebrasoma surgeonfish from the remote Eastern Central Pacific, recognized by its elongated snout, blue pectoral fins, and white peduncle spot. Generally considered reef-safe and best kept singly in spacious systems.
careNotes:
The Black Longnose Tang (Zebrasoma rostratum) is a striking, almost entirely jet-black surgeonfish distinguished by its notably elongated snout, blue pectoral fins, and a small white spot at the caudal peduncle near the scalpels. Adults typically reach about 21–23 cm in length, with FishBase citing 21 cm TL as the recorded maximum.

This species is endemic to the remote Eastern Central Pacific, occurring around the Line, Marquesas, Society and Tuamotu Islands, extending to the Pitcairn Group. It inhabits lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of roughly 8–35 m. Following the closure of Hawaii's commercial aquarium fishery in 2020, specimens in the trade are generally sourced from the Christmas (Kiritimati) Island fishery in Kiribati.

Water quality should be stable and well-oxygenated; conditions reported as appropriate are commonly around 23–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.022–1.025, and dKH 8–12. A 680–760 L (180–200 US gal) or larger reef-style aquarium with plenty of open swimming space is generally advised; a length of around 180 cm gives this active swimmer adequate territory. The species tends to use rockwork crevices for shelter and grazes algae from live rock.

It is generally considered reef-safe — no sources document coral or invertebrate predation, though grazing activity may reduce algae coverage on rockwork. Like other Zebrasoma species it tends to be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped surgeonfishes; it is best kept as the sole tang in the system and added last to an established tank. Has been known to coexist peaceably with most non-surgeonfish reef inhabitants when given space.

All surgeonfish possess paired sharp caudal-peduncle blades ('scalpels') used in defense. These are not venomous but can inflict serious lacerations on handlers and tankmates — care should be taken during transfers, and a tang should never be handled bare-handed or by net only without consideration of the spines. Like most tangs, this species tends to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept in poor water quality or with insufficient vitamin- and algae-rich diet.

Source cross-check notes: Adult size was consistent across sources — LiveAquaria and Aquatics Unlimited cite 9 in (~23 cm), FishBase 21 cm TL, ReefApp ~21 cm. Minimum tank recommendations spread widely: LiveAquaria 180 gal (~681 L), Aquatics Unlimited 200 gal (~757 L), ReefApp 300 L. Per the tang sizing rule, the larger hobby recommendations were favored and rounded to a sensible 700 L for the active-swimming lifestyle. Temperature ranged from 72–78°F (22–26°C) per hobby sources to 23–28°C per FishBase; averaged to 23–26°C. Native-range claims conflicted: Aquatics Unlimited stated 'Hawaii and Christmas Islands' but historic Hawaiian occurrence is not corroborated by FishBase; the species is rangewise confined to the Line, Marquesas, Society, Tuamotu, and Pitcairn Islands per FishBase, with Christmas Island being Kiritimati in the Line Islands.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of this species has not been documented in available sources. Zebrasoma surgeonfish are broadcast spawners in the wild, and successful aquarium reproduction is considered highly unlikely given the tank volumes and pelagic larval stages involved.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species product page for Black Longnose Tang. Provided adult size (9 in), tank minimum (180 gal), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), care level (moderate), herbivore diet, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe status, and behavioral notes. Started from collection URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/tangs-surgeons and located the product via search.

additional.name: aquaticsunlimited.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (9 in / ~23 cm), tank minimum (200 gal, 18+ in width), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), SG (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), semi-aggressive temperament, herbivorous diet, and reef compatibility. Noted Hawaii-fishery closure in 2020 affecting trade supply; range claim of 'Hawaii and Christmas Islands' partially conflicts with FishBase.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Acanthuridae / Acanthuriformes), max size (21 cm TL), depth (8–35 m), temperature range (23–28°C), native distribution (Line, Marquesas, Society, Tuamotu Islands to Pitcairn Group), and benthopelagic reef habitat. IUCN status: Data Deficient (2010 assessment).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (21 cm), reef-safe status, herbivorous algae-grazing diet, and Eastern Central Pacific range. Minimum tank cited as 300 L was substantially lower than other sources; not used per tang sizing rule. Reported salinity ~SG 1.026.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding records found in available sources. Broadcast spawner in the wild; not bred in aquaria.
